title: Pozole Rojo Red Hominy Soup
description: Pozole Rojo is a deeply flavorful, hearty Mexican stew, renowned for its rich red chile broth, tender slow-cooked pork, and plump hominy kernels. Each spoonful offers a comforting blend of savory pork, earthy chiles, and a satisfying chew, perfectly balanced with crisp fresh garnishes. It's a beloved celebratory dish, warming the soul with every vibrant bite.

ingredients.notes: half quartered, half finely diced for garnish
for cooking pork, plus more if needed

instructions.title: Cook the Pork
instructions.text: Place pork shoulder chunks in a large pot or Dutch oven. Add 8 cups of chicken broth or water, half a quartered onion, 4 whole garlic cloves, and bay leaves. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until the pork is very tender and easily shreddable. Skim any foam that rises to the surface during the first 30 minutes. Once tender, remove the pork to a cutting board, shred it using two forks, and set aside. Strain the cooking liquid (broth) and reserve it.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dried Chiles
instructions.text: While the pork cooks, prepare the chiles. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. Toast the guajillo and ancho chiles for 1-2 minutes per side until fragrant, being careful not to burn them. Remove from heat. Place the toasted chiles in a bowl and cover with very hot water. Let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until softened.

instructions.title: Blend the Chile Sauce
instructions.text: Drain the softened chiles, reserving about 1/2 cup of the soaking liquid. In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, the remaining 4 peeled garlic cloves, the other half of the quartered onion, 1 teaspoon of Mexican oregano, 1/2 teaspoon of ground cumin, and 1 cup of the reserved pork broth. Blend until very smooth, adding a little of the reserved chile soaking liquid if needed to achieve a pourable consistency. Strain the chile sauce through a fine-mesh sieve into a bowl to remove any remaining skins or seeds, pressing down on the solids with a spoon to extract all liquid. Discard the solids.

instructions.title: Combine and Simmer the Pozole
instructions.text: Return the reserved pork broth to the large pot. Add the strained chile sauce and bring to a gentle simmer. Add the shredded pork and the drained, rinsed hominy to the pot. Stir well to combine. Season with salt and pepper to taste. If the soup is too thick, add more chicken broth or water to reach your desired consistency.

instructions.title: Develop Flavors
instructions.text: Reduce the heat to low, cover, and let the pozole simmer for at least 30-45 minutes to allow the flavors to meld and deepen. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking. Taste and adjust seasoning as necessary.

instructions.title: Serve with Garnishes
instructions.text: Ladle the hot pozole into individual bowls. Serve immediately with a generous array of garnishes on the side: shredded lettuce, thinly sliced radishes, finely diced white onion, lime wedges, and extra dried Mexican oregano. Offer tostadas for dipping or crumbling into the soup.

tips: For deeper flavor, lightly toast the dried chiles in a dry skillet before rehydrating them. This enhances their aroma and smokiness.
Always strain your chile sauce! This step is crucial for achieving the smooth, velvety texture characteristic of authentic pozole rojo.
Don't skip the garnishes. The fresh crunch of lettuce and radishes, the bite of onion, and the zest of lime are essential for balancing the rich flavors of the soup.
article:
## History of Pozole Rojo Red Hominy Soup

Pozole, a cornerstone of Mexican culinary tradition, boasts a history stretching back to pre-Hispanic times. Originally, it was a ceremonial dish prepared with human flesh during Aztec rituals, a practice that ceased with the Spanish conquest. With the introduction of pork by the Spanish, the dish evolved, and pork became the primary meat, mirroring the texture and symbolism of the original. The word 'pozole' itself derives from the Nahuatl word 'pozolli,' referring to the cacahuazintle corn kernels that swell when cooked. Today, pozole is a beloved dish across Mexico, especially popular during holidays, national celebrations, and family gatherings. Pozole Rojo, with its vibrant red broth, is particularly iconic in states like Jalisco, Guerrero, and Mexico City, a testament to its enduring cultural significance.

## Why This Recipe Works

This Pozole Rojo recipe achieves its authentic, profound flavor through a careful layering of traditional techniques and ingredients. The long, slow simmer of the pork not only ensures melt-in-your-mouth tenderness but also creates a rich, collagen-infused broth that forms the backbone of the soup. The use of rehydrated and blended dried guajillo and ancho chiles provides a complex, earthy, slightly fruity, and smoky depth without overwhelming heat, which is characteristic of genuine Pozole Rojo. Straining the chile sauce is a non-negotiable step that guarantees a silky-smooth, restaurant-quality broth. Finally, the nixtamalized hominy, with its unique chewy texture and ability to absorb the savory broth, completes the dish, making it a perfectly balanced and incredibly satisfying meal that truly represents Mexican culinary heritage.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To elevate your Pozole Rojo, focus on ingredient quality and technique. When selecting pork, a Boston butt (pork shoulder) with good marbling will yield the most tender and flavorful results. For the chiles, don't rush the toasting process; a gentle toast awakens their essential oils, but burning them will result in a bitter flavor. Always use real Mexican oregano, which has a distinct, more robust, and citrusy flavor profile compared to Mediterranean oregano. Taste and adjust seasoning frequently throughout the cooking process, especially after adding the chile sauce and hominy, as flavors will continue to develop. For an extra layer of richness, you can sauté a little diced onion and garlic in lard or oil before adding the strained chile sauce to the pot. Lastly, encourage guests to generously customize their bowls with fresh garnishes – the contrast in textures and temperatures is key to the authentic pozole experience.

## Serving Suggestions

Pozole Rojo is a meal in itself, but its serving presentation is as crucial as its preparation. Traditionally, it is served in large, deep bowls, allowing ample space for the hearty soup and a generous array of fresh garnishes. Present the shredded lettuce, thinly sliced radishes, finely diced white onion, and lime wedges in separate small dishes, allowing each diner to customize their bowl to their preference. A pinch of crumbled dried Mexican oregano sprinkled over the top just before eating adds a final aromatic flourish. Crispy tostadas are an absolute must, either for scooping up the savory broth and hominy or for crumbling directly into the soup. For a complete Mexican feast, a side of fresh avocado slices or a simple salsa verde can complement the rich flavors, though the pozole itself is often the star of the table.

jsonLd.name: Pozole Rojo Red Hominy Soup
jsonLd.description: Pozole Rojo is a deeply flavorful, hearty Mexican stew, renowned for its rich red chile broth, tender slow-cooked pork, and plump hominy kernels. Each spoonful offers a comforting blend of savory pork, earthy chiles, and a satisfying chew, perfectly balanced with crisp fresh garnishes. It's a beloved celebratory dish, warming the soul with every vibrant bite.
jsonLd.keywords: Mexican, Mexican, authentic, traditional, soups, Mexican, authentic, traditional, soups, stews, pork, hominy, chile
recipeIngredient: 3 lbs pork shoulder (Boston butt) (cut into 2-inch chunks)
1 large white onion (half quartered, half finely diced for garnish)
8 cloves garlic (4 whole, 4 peeled for chile sauce)
10-12 dried guajillo chiles (stems and seeds removed)
3-4 dried ancho chiles (stems and seeds removed)
2 (25 oz) cans canned hominy (drained and rinsed)
8 cups chicken broth or water (for cooking pork, plus more if needed)
1 tsp Mexican oregano (plus more for garnish)
1 head shredded lettuce (Romaine or iceberg, for garnish)

recipeInstructions.name: Cook the Pork
recipeInstructions.text: Place pork shoulder chunks in a large pot or Dutch oven. Add 8 cups of chicken broth or water, half a quartered onion, 4 whole garlic cloves, and bay leaves. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until the pork is very tender and easily shreddable. Skim any foam that rises to the surface during the first 30 minutes. Once tender, remove the pork to a cutting board, shred it using two forks, and set aside. Strain the cooking liquid (broth) and reserve it.

recipeInstructions.text: While the pork cooks, prepare the chiles. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. recipeInstructions.text: Drain the softened chiles, reserving about 1/2 cup of the soaking liquid. recipeInstructions.text: Reduce the heat to low, cover, and let the pozole simmer for at least 30-45 minutes to allow the flavors to meld and deepen. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking. Taste and adjust seasoning as necessary.

recipeInstructions.text: Ladle the hot pozole into individual bowls. Serve immediately with a generous array of garnishes on the side: shredded lettuce, thinly sliced radishes, finely diced white onion, lime wedges, and extra dried Mexican oregano. Offer tostadas for dipping or crumbling into the soup.
